headline: Software built
on strong
foundations.
home.body: I am Daniël van Ginneken, a 19-year-old software development student from the Netherlands. My background in system support and infrastructure gave me a practical technical base. Now I am intentionally moving deeper into software engineering.

anchors.label: Software focus
anchors.title: Building comes first
anchors.body: From idea to working product: I build with structure, readability, and maintainability in mind.

anchors.label: Infrastructure roots
anchors.title: Grounded in systems
anchors.body: My earlier IT roles make me think naturally about stability, deployment, and how software behaves in production.

anchors.label: Now
anchors.title: Student and actively building
anchors.body: I am developing projects that show growth: clear in scope, strong in fundamentals, and focused in execution.

featuredProject.name: Portfolio v2
featuredProject.description: A bilingual portfolio experience using a glass-based surface system and editorial pacing.

about.headline: Technical foundation, software as direction.
paragraphs: My first professional steps were in support and infrastructure. That environment taught me how systems are actually used: under pressure, across dependencies, and by people affected immediately by small failures. I carry that perspective into everything I build.

During my software development studies, my focus shifted from reactive fixes to proactive design. I enjoy building solutions that are not only functional but also logical for future developers. To me, good software is clear, traceable, and calm in daily use.

I want to grow into a role where software engineering and product thinking come together. Not just shipping features, but explaining choices: why this architecture, why this trade-off, why this step now. That is how building becomes durable and professional.

takeaways.title: Systems thinking
takeaways.body: I look beyond the code file and factor in infrastructure, reliability, and end-user impact from the start.

takeaways.title: Clear execution
takeaways.body: I work in a structured way, communicate decisions early, and prefer fewer outputs that are solid over many without a foundation.

takeaways.title: Learning mindset
takeaways.body: I make progress visible, ask for targeted feedback, and use it to raise both technical quality and delivery pace.

projects.headline: Projects with quality in focus.
projects.description: Selections of recent builds and concepts currently being expanded.

placeholder.name: Project details incoming
placeholder.description: New projects will appear here as structured rows once they are ready for publication.

journey.headline: From support toward software engineering.
journey.intro: My path moves from operational IT into focused software delivery. Each step has sharpened how I design and build.

band.label: Start
band.title: IT support and operations
band.body: Practical grounding in incidents, systems, and ownership.

band.label: Focus
band.title: Software development studies
band.body: A deliberate shift toward building, modeling, and iterating.

band.label: Direction
band.title: Engineering with foundations
band.body: Quality, clarity, and durable decisions as a baseline.

entries.title: First step in IT support
entries.description: I worked on support requests, basic administration, and user problems. This taught me to recognize failure patterns in systems and become disciplined in structured troubleshooting.
entries.relevance: Relevant because: this is where I learned how essential stability and clear documentation are.

entries.title: Shift toward software development
entries.description: During my studies, my interest moved from operational fixes to design and implementation. I started building personal projects and making technical choices explicit.
entries.relevance: Relevant because: this step connected my technical base with product-oriented thinking.

entries.title: Portfolio and focused builds
entries.description: I now build projects with clear scope, consistent structure, and attention to maintainability. Not only to show what I can build, but also how I work.
entries.relevance: Relevant because: it forms a strong foundation for a professional software engineering role.

contact.intro: Open to internships, junior opportunities, and collaborations where software quality and ownership matter.
